title: fragment #01
lines: Ocean of memories

Whoever named me must have known what I was meant to become. An ocean. An ocean of memories. Some have sunk to my floor, buried thousands of feet beneath. Some still float, refusing to drown. Yet I hold them all the same. Some memories have rusted over time, corroded and broken, now home to the strange life that grows out of decay. Others cling to me like plastic , ever present, poisoning what still tries to live within. The ones drifting near the shore wait for the wind to turn, for the tides to shift and set them free. Sooner or later, they will reach somewhere, finally putting an end to this endless struggle of pull and push. And then there are those lost in between too afraid to sink, too far to reach the land. They drift in silence, nameless, unseen, unheard like me, just trying to belong somewhere, to belong to someone. Maybe that is what we all are, lost souls trapped in our own oceans, calling out to shores that will never call back.

title: fragment #02
lines: An ocean, a pond and all that hurts
“Every soul carries its own quiet ache. Some scream, some whisper, all are real.”

To be human is to suffer. I think that really encapsulates what being a human truly means. Other animals suffer too; however, they are incapable of organized thought and the dread of the future, or the weight of the past. As a lion, your only concern might be how to hunt and how to mate with a lioness. On the other hand, a human has hunger for things that are incomprehensible to animals: a hunger to be loved, a hunger to be recognized, a hunger to be seen. We ache for a sense of belonging, for the warmth of being understood, even if only by a single soul. And that makes us do some really questionable things. Suffering is universal and it can not be measured. There are different views on human suffering by everyone. And there is no comparison. Just because you are drowning in an ocean doesn't mean someone  else can't drown in a pond. Pain does not ask how deep the water is; it simply knows how to pull you under. Some people think their suffering was an act of injustice. They compare their suffering to others and despise them, for they have not suffered the same way. No matter the social status of a person he suffers regardless. Suffering is the one inheritance we all share, though it wears a different face for each of us. Suffering becomes a mirror reflecting not only our fragility but also the choices we make in response to it. It reveals the stories we tell ourselves, the battles we fight in silence, and the weight we choose to carry. Perhaps that is what makes human suffering distinct it is never just about the pain itself, but the meaning we attach to it. And in that meaning we either break, or become something entirely new. In the end, suffering whispers what joy cannot: that we are breakable, but we endure. That we are fragile, yet capable of infinite resilience. That even in our pain, we remain beautifully, unbearably human.

title: fragment #03
lines: Sweat, ache and ghosts

When you are sleep deprived for weeks now and down with a 104°F  fever, your mind plays tricks on you. You can't really tell if she's really standing by the door with her arms open and tears in her eyes, or it's just your mind showing you some good moments before it fades into nothingness. A cruel mercy before the darkness takes over. The air's still and heavy with the smell of sweat and medicine. Her outline glitches in the dim light, as if she's stitched together from memory and regret. You blink and she's gone. No sound, no goodbye, just the echo of warmth where she used to stand. Maybe it is the fever making you remember how it used to feel warm and safe before everything fell apart. You close your eyes, hoping she'll still be there when you open them. But the room only grows quieter, and the shadows keep moving closer. And for a second, you almost wish it wasn't an illusion because if it is, then even your memories have learned how to lie to you. And then you lost the battle and gave in. The room swayed, your mind drowned in its own heat. Sleep came not as rest, but as defeat. Slow, heavy, inevitable. The fever finally closed your eyes for you.

The next morning, you wake up terrified that it really was an illusion and now she's gone for good. A panic spreads through your veins as you try to get up, the emptiness pressing down on your chest. You want to go to her, wherever that is, but your body refuses to obey. Minutes pass like hours before you finally muster the strength to stand. You take one step forward, just one and the world shifts violently before your eyes. The cold and unyielding floor rushes to meet you, and you fall. Then you understand as you're losing consciousness that its the fever taking it's toll. Turning love, loss and delusion into an endless ocean where everything you once loved is drowning. The world fades, the heartbeat slows and for a fleeting second, it almost feels like peace.

title: fragment #04
lines: Who am I?

I had this voice inside me. One I thought was trying to protect me. It never became something sentient. It was simply always there, sitting quietly in the shadows. It was me, yet not me. His thoughts, his way of life, his perception of the world. They were nothing like mine. I can not remember a time when he wasn't there. Sometimes he was nothing more than a whisper in the corner of my mind. Other times, he was louder than my own voice. But always, he waited. For years, I kept him at bay. I told myself I was stronger than the silence, stronger than than him. But when I was eight, in the middle of a storm I couldn't understand. Something that left me small, scared, and shivering in the dark. I remember that day in fragments. How the walls of the house felt too close, how the air tasted like iron, how my little hands trembled but my face remained calm. He took over. And it felt... good. It was as if the bad things weren't happening to me anymore, but to someone else, while I stood as a witness. It felt distant, almost unreal. Like watching a film of my own life, where I no longer played the lead. I condemned him, yet I was grateful for the distance he created between me and the pain. It was wrong, and I knew it yet it was all I could do. At thirteen, I let him take over again. But this time, it was different. He didn't just step in; he asked me to step back. To disappear. To let him hold everything I could not. And slowly without even noticing, that became a habit. A quiet, a dangerous one. Every time the world hurt too much, I handed him the weight while I drifted further from my own life. I remember sitting alone on the school playground after everyone left, tracing shapes in the dust with a stick, pretending everything was fine while I waited for my father to come get me. He was late. I'd hear laughter in the distance, the kind I could never join. And then, the voice would wrap itself around me like a thick blanket, numbing everything. There were nights when I'd lie awake staring at the ceiling, and it felt like two people were breathing inside my chest. One of them hurt. The other was calm. He never cried. He never trembled. He only waited for me to give up the fight. He told me/ showed me that he could make it hurt less. And so I let him. Again and again. Until one day, I could no longer tell where he ended and I began.

I gave him many names. "Sam Frost", "Just7s", "Oceaknight", "pastaMan," and others I have long forgotten. My friends thought it was just a gaming persona. They laughed when I spoke of him like someone real. But he was more than that. He was the quiet part of me that learned how to keep me alive. He saved me, more times than I can count. In the moments when I was too weak to face reality. In the nights when it pulled me back from the ledge. In the silence when it hid every sharp object in the house. He was the one who did not flinch when everything inside me cracked. The one who knew how to breath when I couldn't. And now, I don’t even know who I am. Am I the one who ran? Or the one who took the blows for me? Which of us is real? The quiet, trembling boy, or the shadow that wore my skin when I couldn’t? Have I always been this weak? Or was he just stronger? Somewhere along the way, the line between us blurred, and I can’t tell if I built him… or if he built me.

But I realized too late that while I thought I was being spared, all I was doing was running. That truth came quietly. Not with a scream, not with blood, but with the slow ache of realization. The day I looked in the mirror and didn't recognize the face staring back at me, there was nothing left to save. The reflection was colder, sharper. It looked like someone who had watched too much burn and done nothing to stop it. It was not the reflection that frightened me. It was the calm. It was his calm. When I turned around, there were only ashes. Ashes of the love I had for others. Ashes of the dreams I once held. Ashes of the people I lost along the way. And somewhere among the ashes, he was still standing. Silent, patient, calm, and waiting. He didn't even realize what has he burned. He doesn't need to fight for space anymore. I gave it to him piece by piece, every time I chose escape over pain. And now, he wears my face better than I ever did. He had protected me, yes but he had also taken everything that made me me.

And now, as I stand here, I am learning to face the world without him leading the way. To shoulder the sorrow and grief he carried for me all these years. To feel everything I once hid from. I know he destroyed many things, but I also know he was never my enemy. He was the friend who stood between me and the storm. The guardian who took the blows I couldn't. So, this time I won't erase him. I'll let him rest. I will carry the weight he once bore alone. And maybe that's what growing up really means: not forgetting the shadows that kept us alive, but learning to walk in the light they once shielded us from.

I don’t remember when his voice stopped sounding different from mine. I don’t remember the exact moment when I stepped back, or when he stepped forward. Sometimes I wonder if I ever truly existed, or if I was just a story he told himself to feel less alone. Maybe I was just the mask, and he was the face beneath. Maybe I’ve been the echo all along. The memories don’t help. When I think back, I can’t tell if it was me sitting on that playground, staring at the empty sky, or him. I can’t tell if it was me who felt the sting of the world or if I was just watching from behind a glass wall while someone else lived my life. Who is the real me? Was I always this fragile, or did he make me believe I was? Is he just a fragment of me? or am I just what was left after he carved himself out of my bones?
Who am I?
Am I real?

title: fragment #05
lines: A light that was once earth
It's been so long since I have heard you say my name. The silence aches.

It feels like I am drifting away from earth. Slowly, endlessly into nothingness. With every passing moment I float farther into the dark. I am not in a ship; there is no comfort, no control. Just a suit wrapped around my frail body. Infront of me, a faint glimmer Earth, I think. Or maybe it's just another dying star. I can't even remember which light once meant home. It's so cold. I am so alone. Everywhere I look, there's nothing but darkness. An ocean of ink scattered with flickering stars. They shimmer for a heartbeat and then fade away. Sometimes I reach out, thinking if I could stretch a little farther, I might touch one. I imagine it warm, soft, alive but my hand cuts through emptiness. The silence out here is suffocating. I can hear my own breath echo inside the helmet. Slow, shallow and desperate. Every exhale fogs the visor, a fragile reminder that I am still here. Soon the oxygen will run out. I will stop breathing, stop feeling, stop being. I wonder what my last thoughts will be. Will I see the world I once called home? The green fields under golden sunlight, vast oceans folding into themselves or will I see the rot I left there? Or will it fade into this same dark horizon, until even my memory turns black? I try to remember the sound of rain, the scent of soil after a storm. But everything feels distant now, like it belonged to someone else. The stars I once wanted to explore don't comfort me anymore; they only remind me of how small I am, how far I have fallen from everything that mattered. It hurts knowing you were the one sending me here. It hurts knowing that someday, you'll forget I ever existed. But I won't. How could an astronaut forget the home he'll never see again? Each passing day kills my hope a little and yet I cling to it like the last tether keeping me alive. Hope is all I have. And still the truth burns through the cold; I was the one who poisoned my own world until it could no longer sustain me. And now I am left to drift among the stars, a ghost of my own mistakes. The guilt haunts me in this endless dark. I am becoming a graveyard. Of memories, of regrets, of everything I could have saved.

But what truly kills me is knowing you won't be here when I'm gone. You won't hear my last breath or see my body fade into the dark. I'll vanish quietly. One flicker among the millions. While you go on, unaware that somewhere above, I am still calling your name with lungs that no longer work. Maybe the stars will remember me when you don't. Maybe the last warmth in my chest will be the echo of your voice. But maybe that is what it means to be human. To destroy what we love and mourn it when it's gone. Maybe the stars are not distant at all, but fragments of what we once were. If anyone ever looks up and sees me among them, let them know: I was here once. I loved, I lost, I drifted. And somewhere in the silence, I became part of the sky I could never reach.
And when the oxygen finally ends, I hope my final thought is of you standing on the Earth I ruined, looking up at the night sky, never realizing that the faintest light you see was once me.

title: fragment #06
lines: A familiar/strange world

I have never felt so far, so distant from her than I feel right now sitting on this cold pavement of my college parking space. I don't know the reason but the shade of this familiar tree is colder than usual, the heat of the sun is harsher than usual. Today nothing feels gentle. The ground feels unstable beneath my feet, even the breeze feels like it is pushing me back. The same air I've been breathing since childhood feels heavier, the same road I've been taking for years look longer and the silence that once pleased me feels sharper. People pass by like distant shadows, their laughter cutting through me like a reminder of life that no longer belongs to me. It feels as if the world has moved on overnight and I am the only one left standing still, holding the ruins of what used to be my life. I try to breathe but even the air feels cold and unforgiving like it's punishing me for surviving when everything I cared for didn't. It's strange how fast the world shows its teeth when it realises you have nothing left to protect yourself with. Everything around me looks the same, yet it feels wrong. Hostile. Like the world is quietly celebrating that I finally broke.
Now everything I touch feels distant, everything I see looks faded. Today the world is not just cold. It feels like it is watching me struggle, waiting to see how long before I fall apart completely.

title: fragment #07
lines: It hurts so... to let somebody go
I wanted you to stay.

Not in the dramatic way people talk about. No grand promises, no desperate hands grabbing at your sleeve. I wanted you to stay in the quiet ways. In the way mornings would still recognize us. In the way your name wouldn't feel like a bruise when it crossed my mind. But wanting is a fragile thing. It bends too easily under reality. I learned that love doesn't always end with anger. Sometimes it ends with understanding arriving too late. With silence that isn't cruel, just final. With two people realizing that holding on would only turn what was once gentle into something sharp.
So I let you go...

Not because I stopped loving you, but because loving you meant refusing to become a place you felt trapped inside. There are nights I still reach for the version of you that stayed. The one that laughed a second longer. The one that almost chose me. I hold that version carefully, like something already broken. I don't ask it to live, only to remind me that I tried.
Letting you go wasn't brave.
It was heavy. It was slow. It was choosing loneliness over resentment, absence over damage. It was trusting that some connections are meant to change us, not remain with us.

But no one tells you what happens after you let go. They don’t tell you that the world doesn’t reward you for choosing peace. That doing the right thing doesn’t soften the nights. That your hands still ache for something they are no longer allowed to touch. I walk through my days carrying the weight of a choice that looks noble from the outside and feels like rot on the inside. I smile at conversations that don’t know you. I laugh in rooms where your absence is louder than any sound. I learn, over and over, how quiet a heart can become when it has nothing left to argue for. I learned the Geography of loss. Which streets I avoid, which songs I abandon halfay through, which hours of the night are sharpest. I learned how to edit my life around the outline you left, like trimming a photo until the missing parts looks intentional. Some mornings I wake up empty before I wake up sad. The sadness comes later. Polite and through, checking every room you once were in, reminding me of what will never knock again. I tell people I'm fine as language has limits. Because there is no word for the dull ache of choosing you and still feeling punished for it. Because admitting the truth would require explaining how love can end and still keep taking things with it. I don't miss you loudly anymore. I miss you in small and cruel ways. In reflexes, in my half formed thoughts, in the instinct to turn and tell you something before remembering there is no one there to hear it. And with that quiet comes a deeper kind of sadness. Not sharp enough to demand attention, not loud enough to be rescued. It settles instead, like dust on unused furniture, like evening light fading without a ceremony. It makes a home in the corner of my days, in pauses between thoughts, in the space where your name is expected but will never arrive. This sadness doesn't ask to be healed. It asks to be lived with. TO be acknowledged without solutions, without hope forced into it's mouth. I move slower inside it. I choose words more carefully.
ANd maybe this is how it stays. Not as heartbreak, but as a constant dimming. A life lived in the darkness. A heart that loved deeply once, and now knows the cost of remembering how.

title: fragment #08
lines: I hope
Hope is a strange thing.

Some days it sits softly on your chest, like a small light reminding you that not everything is ruined beyond repair. It whispers that maybe, just maybe, things will turn out different this time. It keeps your hands moving when your heart wants to stop. It keeps your pulse steady when the world around you is collapsing. On the good days, hope feels like a quiet pat on your back, urging you forward without demanding anything in return. It feels like the first breath after drowning. It pulls sunlight through the cracks you didn’t know were still in you. It shows you glimpses of the person you could become, the life that might still be waiting, the softness you thought you’d forever lost. On those days, hope is the only thing that makes you believe you deserve more than what hurt you. It’s the strength in your voice when you’re trembling. It’s the reason you choose to wake up again, even when the night has taken too much from you.
But other days…

Hope feels like a chain you never agreed to wear. It keeps you waiting in empty rooms, staring at doors that will never open, cling to moments that won’t return, replay promises that were never meant to be kept. It convinces you to hold on long after your fingers have started to bleed. It drags you back into the same dreams that have already broken you once, pulling you under again with the promise of “almost”. It makes you rehearse futures that will never come, and grieve losses that haven’t happened but feel real enough to tear something inside you each time. And the worst part is how it prolongs the suffering. How it keeps you reaching for things that keep drifting further away. How every disappointment feels sharper because you dared to believe. Hope becomes a slow poison. Sweet enough to keep you drinking, lethal enough to killing you from inside.

And you don’t know which version to believe. You don’t know if hope is saving you or slowly killing you. You stand between the light it offers and the darkness it creates, unsure of which side you belong to. Some night you want to let go of it entirely, drop it like a weight you’ve carried too long. But the moment you try, some faint warmth flickers in your chest again. A stubborn reminder, that a part of you still wants to believe. Some days you reach for it desperately, and some days you wish you could rip it out of your chest just to breathe without the ache. You keep asking yourself if you’re being brave or just foolish, if you’re fighting or simply refusing to let the truth in. You wonder if you’re loyal to something real, or clinging to a ghost you created out of loneliness. Every thought contradicts the next. Every feeling overlaps the one before it. Letting go feels like giving up. Holding on feels like breaking down. And you can’t tell which pain is the one you’re meant to choose. Some nights the confusion grows so loud you cover your ears and sit in a corner of your room. Your chest tightens, every breath you take feels like the air is passing through your lungs, your mind spirals through the versions of your life that could exist depending on which way you lean. You’re scared that hope is leading you somewhere beautiful. You’re scared that hope is leading you nowhere at all. And the worst part is the not knowing.
Maybe that’s the curse of hoping. It never lets you fully stay; it never lets you fully leave.

title: fragment #09
lines: I became you mother

I became you, Ma, without ever wanting to. Your anger lives in my blood, your silence fills my lungs, your silence bends around my ribs like something inherited, unavoidable. Your voice lives inside me, sharp and cold, shaping the way I speak to myself. Your loneliness leaks through my bones, pulses through my veins. Your disappointment sank into my skin until it became my own reflection. I became the shape of your suffering, the echo of a woman who never learned how to live without pain. I carry the weight of your unfinished wars on my arm, battles that were never mine to fight yet somehow became my daily ritual. I flinch at the site of joy just as you did, suspicious of anything that comes too easily. I brace myself for loss even in moments of safety, because you taught me that nothing ever stays. I hear your sighs leave my mouth. I feel your resignation settle into my shoulders. And in those moments, I am terrified of how familiar my misery feels. How natural it has become to hurt without asking why. Your wounds stitched themselves into my choices, into the way I trust, into the way I leave before I can be left. I confuse endurance with strength because that's all I ever saw you practice. I confuse silence with dignity because you taught me that survival is quiet. Some nights, I swear I can feel your sorrow sitting beside me, breathing with me, waiting for me to break in the same places you did. And maybe I already have. Maybe the cracks in me are just old fractures passed down through bloodlines, repeating themselves with cruel precision. I look at myself and see the ruins of your dreams carved into my face.

I became you, Ma, in all the ways you prayed I wouldn't. In all the ways that destroyed you. And the tragedy is I don't know if I'm becoming you out of love, or because I never learned another way to survive. I became you, Ma.
But I am still trying to become someone who learns how to live.

title: fragment #10
lines: Tick tock tick tock

It's 2:28 in the night and I can't seem to fall asleep. The silence is deafening. With every tick of the clock, the night deepens. With every breath I take, I hear the darkness swallowing the world entirely. It's been so long since I've known this much silence that I can hear my own breathing. This breathing that weighs more than it should. Each inhale feels borrowed, temporary, like my lungs no longer know what to do with air. Every ounce I take in passes straight through me, leaving nothing behind. No relief. No fullness. Just an empty unsatisfied ache that convinces me I am not really breathing at all. As the feeling settles in, the quiet panic of not being able to breathe makes me try harder. As if effort alone could fix whatever is breaking inside me. But it's all in vain. The deeper I breathe, the emptier I feel. Like I'm hollowing myself out from the inside, turning into a shell that echoes with every heartbeat. It makes me want to tear open my chest and squeeze my heart as hard as I can, just to remind it that it's still there, that it's still mine. My heart races as if I am running. Fleeing something unseen. Yet here I lie unmoving on this cold bed that offers no comfort. The sheets cling to me like they know I won't be going anywhere. The ceiling stares back, indifferent as my thoughts drift towards the one person I should have forgotten by now. But memory doesn't listen to reason. It arrives uninvited and sits beside me in the dark. Her absence fills the room more completely than her presence did. Every silence sounds like her name. Every pause feels like a conversation that never got an ending. I close my eyes, hoping sleep will take me out of my misery for a while. But the night refuses. It keeps me awake, trapped in this space between breath and memory where nothing hurts loudly but everything hurts constantly.
And the clock keeps ticking.
tick-tock tick-tock...

title: fragment #11
lines: With this treasure I summon you "Red"

I love you in a way that has nowhere to go anymore. It exists without my permission, without a future, without a place to rest. Once, I was the reason you felt safe. Later, I became the reason you learned how to leave. That is the truth I can not soften, no matter how gently I hold it. Love does not erase damage. Time does not unbreak what was broken by choice. I know now that fate is not always cruel by accident. Sometimes it simply refuses to undo what we have already done. We are not kept apart because the love wasn’t real, we are kept apart because it was not enough to survive what I caused. And this is the punishment I carry quietly: To love you without reaching for you, to wish for you without asking, to protect you now by staying away. Letting you go would be easier if love had died. But it hasn’t. It stays, tender and useless, reminding me that what hurts most is not losing you, it is knowing that the love is still here, and the harm is too. So I will do the only honest thing left to me. I will not rewrite the past. I will not beg the future. I will leave you untouched by my wanting. If fate has any mercy at all, let it be this, that you are lighter without me, and that my love, finally, learns how to let you go. I am learning how to let go, though my heart still resists the lesson. I know what I broke. I know love does not grant absolution, and that some wounds stay open no matter how sincerely regret is spoken. We are not apart because the feeling wasn’t real. We are apart because reality still remembers what our heart wishes it could forget. Letting you go means unlearning a future I had already lived inside my mind. I had placed you in moments that never arrived, quiet mornings, shared silences, a life that felt inevitable once.  I wanted it to stay, God how I wanted it to stay, I wanted the world to pause right there, before the damage, before the leaving, before love learned how to hurt. I begged whatever gods would listen to make it last, to give us more time, to let the good outweigh what I had done wrong, but prayers do not rewrite consequences. I cried until my chest ached and then cried some more, the kind of crying that empties you and still finds something left to take, the kind that does not ask to be witnessed . Now I have to gently remove you from places where you still belong to me. There are days I succeed. I tell myself this is mercy, that distance is the last kindness I can offer you. I step back, I stay silent, I let the world continue without my interference. I call this acceptance, and sometimes it almost feels like peace. But hope is stubborn. It lives in the smallest spaces, in the thought that time might soften what it once hardened, that growth might reach where apologies could not. It whispers that maybe fate is not done with us, only careful, only slow. I do not wait for you. I do not reach for you. But somewhere, quietly, I believe that if our paths ever cross again, it will be in a gentler season. That we will meet as people who have learned how to hold without hurting, how to love without breaking. Until then, I will keep learning how to let you go, not by erasing you, but by trusting that if love ever finds us again, it will arrive kinder than it did before. I will keep letting you go in pieces, even though it costs me everything, because loving you means accepting that I am the reason we could not stay.

title: fragment #12
lines: A suffering that never ends

This is the tyranny of always having an unwanted companion beside you. It sits when you sit, it eats when you eat. It doesn't let you sleep. It doesn't let you enjoy the things that once brought you joy. It always stays beside you, yet it never listens. Maybe it appeared when things started to fall apart. Or maybe you created it along the way because you couldn't bear the loneliness. It has been so long now that you can't even recall, even if you try. It stayed dormant when you could still tell the difference between the color of the leaves and color of the sky. But the moment dark clouds appeared, it lifted its head again. Most nights you beg for it to go away, yet it never leaves. It doesn't hear. It doesn't speak. It never has to. Yet you understand everything. Just this morning you rose from bed after another sleepless night, your eyes feeling as if someone were hanging from your eyelids and a nail was being driven into your head with a hammer. Irritated, you walk out of your room and into the balcony where a cool breeze eases you a little. The sun hasn't yet peeked from behind the building it always rises from, but its bright orange rays have already begun to light up the sky. The sight calms you a little. It takes you back to a tie when you weren't aware of its existence. The sudden rustling of leaves pulls you out of nostalgia and back into the present. The trees, as tall as the buildings and some even taller, sway with the wind as if they had been waiting for morning to arrive. You lean against the wall beside you and, for a moment, feel almost happy. For a brief moment you forget about its existence, and about the agony you endured the night before. After a while your feet begin to ache and you go back inside. As you sit on your bed, it sits beside you. The moment you look at its face, it feels as though it hands you back the weight you had briefly forgotten you were carrying. It reminds you of how much you have lost, and how much you are still going to lose. You try to dismiss these thoughts. You take deep breaths and tell yourself to be calm, to be happy. But all you can hear is the screaming of the things you have lost. All the versions of your past stand before you again, each one asking how you lost the thing they cherished more than they ever cherished themselves.
One asks about the action figure you lost.
One asks about the friend who died.
One asks about the promise you broke.
And one asks about the person you lost.

You sit there silently, with no answers and no courage to look at any of them. You wait for them to vanish. You tell yourself it’s just something your mind has conjured, but nothing changes. They sit patiently, waiting for you to answer. The more time passes, the closer they get. Before you know it they are shaking you, pulling you from every direction, demanding an answer. You want to cry and tell them that losing all of it hurt you too. But you are too afraid they will blame you. And that is something you are truly frightened of. Somewhere inside you already believe it was all your fault. But if you hear it from them, it will become real. It will become undeniable.
You have no place to fall.

No matter how you look at it, no matter what everyone tells you, you know you are different. You know in your heart that you are not allowed to fall. There is no ground beneath you to fall onto. Only an endless abyss. No matter how many times you try to persuade yourself that you are allowed to cry, that you are allowed to break, you know it’s all a lie. So you ignore them. You sit there staring at your feet while they try to make you look at them. You feel its hand rest on yours, as if trying to comfort you. But the hand is cold. Colder and more distant than everything else. You ask yourself how many years it has been since you last lowered your sword and shield. Your hands are tired. They tremble. Yet you hold on to them for dear life. Oh, how you wish you could just rest for a night. A night filled with nothing but a warm embrace and a sleep deep enough to silence every voice. After that you would never ask for rest again. But it stays. All of it vanishes in an instant when the alarm rings.
Everything except it.

You take deep breaths as though you had been underwater for far too long. As you reach for your phone, it reaches for the phone with you. You are reminded of what a notification once meant. A while ago, the ringing of the phone might have meant a call from that certain someone. You throw the phone onto the pillow before the memories can pull you under. But it picks up the phone and brings it back to you. You close your eyes in horror, yet you can still see it unlocking your phone and scrolling through your gallery. You scream "no" inside your head, but the visions refuse to stop. And there it is.
The image of someone you cannot forget.
The image of someone who forgot you long ago.

You open your eyes and find yourself somewhere else. Somewhere very familiar, yet you cannot recall where. You look around and see that person walking toward you. You try to run. You try to hide. The street bends. The buildings twist. And suddenly you are standing exactly where you started, facing them again. Once again, you are forced to relive the memory that haunts you. In the corner of the memory, it is still there.
Watching you. Patient.
With empty eyes.

You often think about how to get rid of it. Sometimes your thoughts wander back to old habits, when sharp blades and full sleeves kept you company longer than anyone else ever did. You get up from your bed and search the drawers for blades. Then you remember another promise you made: to leave that habit behind. You try to calm yourself and collapse onto the floor. You cover your ears, hoping the voices will finally stop, but they are stubborn. They refuse to leave you yet. You know this will last for a few more minutes before they disappear again for a while. But the pain they cause is unbearable. You lie there on the cold, hard floor wishing for that warm embrace and that peaceful sleep. But the only warmth you feel is the red puddle spreading beside you.
You wished for warmth.
You got it.
